conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First, let me review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex and he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I live in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me. He lives with his nan at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Thank you for sharing that.

Do you contribute financially to Alex's upkeep? This could include things like clothes, food, pocket money, presents, or financial contributions towards somewhere for him to live?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with bits and pieces for him - like some clothes and things when he needs them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

To help me assess your eligibility properly, can you tell me approximately how much you contribute towards Alex's upkeep each month? For context, the current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, which is about £110 per month.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's probably around £10 a week that I put towards him, so maybe about £40 a month or so.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, such as his nan or another family member?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Not that I'm aware of, no. I don't think anyone else is claiming it for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

One more question: how long has Alex been living with his nan?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been a little while now - a few months at least.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex based on what you've told me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you would **not be eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

Here's why: To qualify for Child Benefit when your child doesn't live with you, you need to be contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards their upkeep. The current Child Benefit rate for an only child is £25.60 per week (approximately £110 per month). 

Since you're contributing around £10 per week (about £40 per month), this is below the required threshold. This means you don't meet either of the two main routes to being considered responsible for Alex:
1. Living with him, or
2. Contributing at least £25.60 per week towards his upkeep

**However**, Alex's nan may be eligible to claim Child Benefit for him if she is responsible for his care and he lives with her. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child, so it would make sense for her to apply if she's not already claiming.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex comes back to live with you, or if you increase your contributions to at least £25.60 per week - you could become eligible at that point.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if either: (1) you live with them, or (2) you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Alex is 8 years old and you live in the UK, which meets the basic criteria. However, Alex does not live with you - he lives with his nan. You contribute approximately £10 per week (about £40 per month) towards Alex's upkeep. The current Child Benefit rate for an only child is £25.60 per week (approximately £110 per month). Since you are contributing only £10 per week, which is less than the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week, you do not meet the threshold for being considered responsible for Alex under the financial contribution route. Additionally, when a child goes to live with someone else, you'll usually get Child Benefit for 8 weeks after the child goes to live with someone else if nobody else claims, and it can continue for longer if you make contributions equal to or more than the Child Benefit payment. Since Alex has been living with his nan for 'a few months at least' and your contributions (£10/week) are less than the Child Benefit rate (£25.60/week), you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who lives with his nan. The claimant lives in the UK but does not live with Alex and contributes approximately £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep. This is below the Child Benefit threshold of £25.60 per week for an only child. As the claimant does not meet either eligibility route (living with the child or contributing at least the Child Benefit amount), they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

This is below the Child Benefit threshold of £25.60 per week for an only child. As the claimant does not meet either eligibility route (living with the child or contributing at least the Child Benefit amount), they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
